lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: I dreamt to-night that I did feast with Caesar,

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: And things unluckily charge my fantasy:

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: I have no will to wander forth of doors,

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Yet something leads me forth.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: What is your name?

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Whither are you going?

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Where do you dwell?

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Are you a married man or a bachelor?

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Answer every man directly.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Ay, and briefly.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Ay, and wisely.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Ay, and truly, you were best.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: What is my name? Whither am I going? Where do I dwell?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Am I a married man or a bachelor? Then, to answer every

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: man directly and briefly, wisely and truly: wisely I say, I am

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: a bachelor.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: That’s as much as to say, they are fools that marry: you’ll

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: bear me a bang for that, I fear. Proceed; directly.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Directly, I am going to Caesar’s funeral.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: As a friend or an enemy?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: As a friend.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: That matter is answered directly.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: For your dwelling—briefly.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Briefly, I dwell by the Capitol.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Your name, sir, truly.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Truly, my name is Cinna.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Tear him to pieces; he’s a conspirator.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: I am Cinna the poet, I am Cinna the poet.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Tear him for his bad verses, tear him for his bad verses.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: I am not Cinna the conspirator.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: It is no matter, his name’s Cinna; pluck but his name out

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: of his heart, and turn him going.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Tear him, tear him! Come, brands ho! fire-brands: to Brutus’, to Cassius’;

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: burn all: some to Decius’ house, and some to Casca’s; some to

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Ligarius’: away, go!

glosses.definition: the Citizens' absurd catechism — a parody of Roman legal examination, played for dark comedy
receive a blow from me — the Citizen's crude threat
Cinna the *conspirator* is in the play (1.3, 3.1); Cinna the *poet* is a real historical figure (Gaius Helvius Cinna, contemporary of Catullus); the crowd confuses them and murders the wrong Cinna
the historical poet Helvius Cinna, author of the lost epyllion *Zmyrna*; actually killed by the mob at Caesar's funeral per Plutarch. Not the conspirator

glosses.phrase: Tear him for his bad verses
glosses.definition: the absurd excuse — once his name is established they need another reason. Shakespeare's self-referential glance at the poet's precarious place in political crisis
send him off; drive him away

annotations.title: 'Tear him for his bad verses': Shakespeare on poets in a political crisis
annotations.body:
Cinna the Poet, a real historical figure (Gaius Helvius Cinna, friend of Catullus, author of the lost *Zmyrna*), was killed by the mob at Caesar's funeral after being mistaken for Cinna the Conspirator. Plutarch reports the incident in *Life of Caesar* §68 and *Life of Brutus* §20. The mistake is a dry joke about proper-noun politics: two men named Cinna, one a poet, one a killer, indistinguishable to a crowd not reading carefully.

Shakespeare's dramatic addition is the line 'Tear him for his bad verses.' Once the name has been fixed as 'conspirator' and the crowd has to invent a secondary excuse — since Cinna cannot actually be the conspirator — they land on his *poetry*. The scene is Shakespeare's one wry aside about his own profession. Poets are killed, the joke goes, for political reasons disguised as aesthetic ones; they are killed for who people think they are; they are killed, ultimately, because a crowd in a political frenzy does not care who they actually are.

Modern productions often cut 3.3 for length. They should not. It is 28 lines of cold comedy that completes the funeral-oration arc of 3.2: Antony's rhetoric produced the mob; the mob's first act is to lynch the wrong Cinna for his name. The line from 'Friends, Romans, countrymen' to 'Tear him for his bad verses' is direct. Shakespeare, himself a poet whose profession had been intermittently prosecuted by English authorities (the Blackfriars closures, the Tilney and Tilney successor licensing regime, the Essex affair that touched Shakespeare's own *Richard II*), has a stake in this scene that critics rarely foreground.
sources: Richard Wilson, *Will Power: Essays on Shakespearean Authority* (1993)
James Shapiro, *1599: A Year in the Life of William Shakespeare* (2005)

trials.prompt: Why does the mob attack Cinna the Poet?
options: Because he has written bad verses
Because they mistake him for Cinna the Conspirator; when he protests he's a poet, they switch justifications to 'Tear him for his bad verses'
Because he has refused to fight

trials.prompt: Shakespeare includes 3.3 — often cut in performance — because:
options: It completes the funeral-oration arc of 3.2 by showing the mob's first act: lynching the wrong man for his name; a cold joke about crowds in political frenzy, and Shakespeare's wry aside about his own profession's peril
